_meta.description: Sample partner sync dataset — illustrates the data shape exchanged between mom's device and partner's device. Keys map directly to localStorage keys used by app.js and partner.js.

checkins.note_text: Baby arrived. Exhausted but she latched on the very first try. Colostrum coming through.
Baby keeps falling asleep mid-feed. Tickling her feet helps a bit. Skin-to-skin all morning.
checkins.voice_transcript: She's sleepy but feeds are going okay. Trying to keep her awake.

checkins.note_text: Milk came in hard. Breasts feel like rocks. Cried twice today — not sure why exactly. Just overwhelmed.
checkins.voice_transcript: I feel so full and it hurts. Baby is struggling to latch when I'm this engorged.

checkins.note_text: Engorgement a bit better after reverse pressure softening. Nipples very sore though. Using lanolin.
Baby cluster feeding from 5pm to 9pm. Growth spurt? I barely moved from the couch. Drank 3 glasses of water.
checkins.voice_transcript: She fed for four hours straight this evening. My arms are tired but she seems satisfied now.

checkins.note_text: Best night so far — 3 hour stretch at 1am. Nipples still sore but latch felt deeper today. Positive.
One week! Engorgement gone. Latch is so much better. Baby back above birth weight at check-up.
checkins.voice_transcript: Doctor said she's doing great. Weight is back up. I feel more confident today.

partner_notes.note: She was incredible today. Took the baby the moment visiting hours ended so she could sleep. Couldn't believe how calm she stayed during labour.
Brought home from hospital. Made moong dal khichdi per the app. She ate well. Baby had 8 feeds.
Hard day — milk came in and she was really struggling. I took the baby for a walk at 8pm so she could shower and rest. Reminded her she's doing amazingly.
Picked up lanolin and extra breast pads. Made sure she had warm soup at lunch and dinner. She seemed calmer this evening.
Cluster feeding evening — I stayed right beside her with water and snacks. Took baby for morning carry so she slept 90 mins uninterrupted.
She had a decent night! 3 hour block. Mood visibly better in the morning. I handled the 6am feed so she could sleep to 8.
One week! Doctor visit went well. Baby above birth weight. Priya is so much more confident. Celebrated with her favourite gulab jamun.

partner_recommendations.headline: She may need a doctor today
partner_recommendations.summary: She's flagged a symptom that can signal mastitis — a breast infection that needs prompt treatment. Don't wait to see if it improves on its own.
actions: Gently ask if she has fever, chills, or a hot red patch on her breast
If yes, help arrange a same-day doctor call or visit — mastitis needs antibiotics quickly
Keep the baby breastfeeding as normal — stopping feeding can make the infection worse
Prepare warm compresses and ensure she's drinking water constantly
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "I'm right here. Let's call the doctor together — getting help fast is the right thing to do."

partner_recommendations.headline: She's in breast pain — act on this today
partner_recommendations.summary: Engorgement or a blocked duct is very painful and can worsen into mastitis if not managed. Your practical support today matters.
actions: Prepare a warm compress (damp warm cloth) — have it ready before feeds
After feeds, bring a cold pack or chilled cabbage leaf for 10 minutes to reduce swelling
Keep her well hydrated — dehydration makes engorgement worse
Don't let feeds get too far apart — frequent breastfeeding is the treatment
If a hard lump doesn't soften after 24 hours, suggest she call her doctor
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "I've got the warm cloth ready whenever you need it. Tell me when she's about to feed."

partner_recommendations.headline: She's emotionally overwhelmed — your presence is the medicine
partner_recommendations.summary: She's flagged feeling tearful or overwhelmed. This is very common in the first two weeks but your response right now shapes how she copes.
actions: Take the baby for at least 30–60 minutes so she has unstructured time to herself
Don't try to fix it or offer advice — just sit close and say 'I see how hard you're working'
Make warm food and bring it without being asked — a small act that signals you're paying attention
Reduce noise, visitors, and stimulation in the house for the rest of the day
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "You don't have to hold it together right now. I've got the baby. Just rest."

partner_recommendations.headline: Sore nipples — small actions that make a real difference
partner_recommendations.summary: Cracked nipples are one of the most common reasons moms stop breastfeeding early. A few things from you today can help her push through.
actions: Check if there's lanolin cream at home — if not, pick some up today
After each feed, remind her to let a little milk air-dry on the nipple before covering
Help her find a comfortable feeding position — a nursing pillow reduces nipple strain
If pain is severe at every single feed, suggest booking a lactation consultant call
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "You're doing the hard part. I'll get the lanolin — just tell me where you last saw it."

partner_recommendations.headline: Cluster feeding tonight — set up her station and stay close
partner_recommendations.summary: Cluster feeding is exhausting but completely normal — the baby is building up milk supply. Your role is to keep her fuelled, hydrated, and not alone.
actions: Set up a feeding station: full water bottle, snack, phone charger, burp cloth — all within arm's reach
Take over all non-feeding tasks for the evening: nappy changes, settling, kitchen, everything
Cook or order warm food — she burns extra calories during marathon feeding sessions
Stay in the room. You don't need to talk — your presence stops her feeling isolated
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "Tell me what you need. I'm on everything else tonight — you just focus on feeding her."

partner_recommendations.headline: She's worried about milk supply — stay calm and be practical
partner_recommendations.summary: Supply worry is common and often unfounded, but the anxiety itself can reduce production. Your calm, practical support matters more than you think.
actions: Don't suggest formula unless she brings it up — it can feel like you're giving up on her
Make sure she's eating warm, nourishing food: methi (fenugreek), oats, dal, and soups all support supply
Reduce stress in the house — keep it quiet, take phone calls elsewhere, hold off visitors
Encourage feeding on demand rather than by clock — frequent feeding builds supply
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "I can see how much you care about this. You're doing everything right. Let me make sure you eat well today."

partner_recommendations.headline: Sleepy baby at the breast — help her stay patient
partner_recommendations.summary: A sleepy baby who won't stay awake to feed is frustrating. Help her keep the environment right and track that the baby is still getting enough.
actions: Keep the room bright and slightly cool during feed times to help baby stay awake
Skin-to-skin helps — suggest undressing baby to just a nappy against her skin
After feeds, count wet nappies together — 6+ per day means baby is getting enough
If baby won't wake for a feed after 4 hours, help her gently rouse the baby (undress, tickle feet)
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "How many wet nappies today? Let's count together — I think it's going better than it feels."

partner_recommendations.headline: She's tired — protect her rest above everything else today
partner_recommendations.summary: No symptoms flagged but she's feeling tired or rough. Rest is the single most important thing right now — it affects milk supply, mood, and recovery.
actions: Take the baby for one full uninterrupted block today — even 90 minutes of sleep is restorative
Handle all household noise and visitors so she can rest without interruption
Bring warm food and a drink without waiting to be asked
One 'you're doing so well' goes further than you'd expect
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "Go sleep. I've got her. I'll bring food when you wake up."

partner_recommendations.headline: A steady day — keep the conditions that got her here
partner_recommendations.summary: She's doing okay today with no symptoms. These stable days are important. Your job is to maintain the environment that helped her get here.
actions: Keep the routine consistent: meals on time, water available, noise low
Offer to take the baby after the next feed so she can nap
Check in verbally once — a simple 'how are you really feeling?' is enough
Plan tomorrow's food now so she doesn't have to think about it
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "You seem more settled today. What do you need from me to keep that going?"

partner_recommendations.headline: She's having a good day — celebrate it and sustain it
partner_recommendations.summary: She's logged a positive mood and no symptoms. Acknowledge it — being seen on a good day is just as meaningful as being supported on a bad one.
actions: Tell her you noticed she seems better today — it means a lot to be seen
Ask if there's something small she'd enjoy (a favourite food, a short walk, a bath alone)
Don't let her take on too much just because she feels better — rest is still important
This is a good moment to talk about the next few days: meals, sleep plan, any upcoming concerns
partner_recommendations.what_to_say: "You look so much better today. I'm really proud of you — what would make today feel even better?"
